edges.description: Loss of CSB, XPD, XPG, or ERCC1-XPF function abolishes the downstream TC-NER complex required for processing transcription- stalling lesions.
Without functional TC-NER, transcription-blocking lesions persist and accumulate in active genes.
Persistent stalled transcription in proliferating neural progenitors impairs neuronal differentiation and neurogenesis.
Persistent stalled transcription in post-mitotic neurons drives progressive neurodegeneration.

nodes.description: Persistent unrepaired bulky DNA adducts and oxidative lesions stall RNA polymerase II on the template strand of actively transcribed genes, preventing transcription elongation. The high transcriptional demand of neural progenitors and developing neurons makes these cells especially vulnerable.

evidence.reference_title: The role of transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair (TC-NER) during mammalian forebrain development.
evidence.snippet: Neural progenitor cells and differentiating neurons in the developing forebrain sustain high transcriptional activity and metabolic demand and are therefore vulnerable to DNA damage.

nodes.description: The TC-NER complex (CSA/CSB → UVSSA-USP7 → TFIIH-XPD → XPG → ERCC1-XPF) is impaired when any required component is missing or destabilised. Damage in actively transcribed DNA can no longer be efficiently removed. COFS shares this defect with Cockayne syndrome (see Cockayne_Syndrome.yaml); the shared biology is mirrored here rather than inherited via `conforms_to` because dismech modules are reserved for cross-disorder conserved mechanisms.

evidence.snippet: In COFS syndrome, as in Cockayne syndrome, DNA repair is impaired in the transcription-coupled NER pathway, but not in the global genome NER pathway.

evidence.reference_title: The role of transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair (TC-NER) during mammalian forebrain development.
evidence.snippet: Transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair is a specialized DNA repair pathway that helps to mitigate damage induced by 'bulky' adducts such as UV-induced pyrimidine dimers and monoadducts formed by reactive oxygen species.

nodes.description: Transcriptional arrest in proliferating neural progenitors impairs neuronal differentiation and neurogenesis, reducing neuron production. This developmental arm produces the microcephaly and cerebellar hypoplasia of COFS.

evidence.reference_title: The role of transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair (TC-NER) during mammalian forebrain development.
evidence.snippet: TC-NER safeguards genome integrity and plays an essential role in neuronal differentiation, synaptogenesis, and neurogenesis.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic loss-of-function variants in one of four NER genes (ERCC6/CSB, ERCC2/XPD, ERCC5/XPG, or ERCC1) eliminate or destabilise the protein product, removing its contribution to transcription-coupled NER. COFS represents the severe prenatal-onset end of the NER-deficiency allelic spectrum.

evidence.snippet: All mutations have been found in Cockayne syndrome gene, CSB, xeroderma pigmentosum genes, XPD and XPG and ERCC1 gene involved in the transcription-coupled NER pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Heterogeneity and overlaps in nucleotide excision repair disorders.
evidence.snippet: Deficiencies in NER typically result in a group of heterogeneous distinct disorders ranging from the mild UV sensitive syndrome to the cancer-prone xeroderma pigmentosum and the neurodevelopmental/progeroid conditions trichothiodystrophy, Cockayne syndrome and cerebro-oculo-facio-skeletal-syndrome.

nodes.description: Transcriptional arrest in post-mitotic neurons drives progressive neurodegeneration of surviving neurons. This degenerative arm contributes to the severe progressive psychomotor decline of COFS, distinct from the developmental neurogenesis deficit.

evidence.reference_title: The role of transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair (TC-NER) during mammalian forebrain development.
evidence.snippet: Impaired TC-NER pathway activity manifests in tissue-level pathologies, including neurodegeneration and increased susceptibility to neurological deficits.
